planting.note: Propagate from stem cuttings — rooted cuttings establish rapidly in warm, moist soil. Plant in spring after frost risk passes in zone 9 and grow as a perennial; the fleshy stems root easily at nodes wherever they contact moist soil. No seed is typically available commercially.

directions: Grow in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained soil; Okinawan spinach thrives in the heat and humidity of zone 9 summers when cool-season greens have long since bolted. The dark green leaves with purple undersides are harvested young and eaten raw in salads, lightly stir-fried, or blended into smoothies; they have a mild, slightly mucilaginous texture and are rich in antioxidants. Pinch growing tips regularly to keep plants bushy and productive; the sprawling stems root at nodes when they touch moist soil, making propagation effortless. This species is distinct from Okinawan sweet potato — it is a member of the daisy family and produces small yellow flowers.
sources: University of Hawaii Cooperative Extension — Tropical Leafy Greens
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Alternative Leafy Vegetables

planting.note: In zone 9 direct sow in fall (September–November) for a winter through spring harvest, or in late winter (February–March) for a spring crop. Bunching onions do not form bulbs; they are harvested as green onions at any stage and regrow continuously from the base if the roots are left in place.

directions: Direct sow thinly 1/4 inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil in fall or late winter; thin to 3–4 inches as seedlings grow, using thinnings as your first scallion harvest. Unlike common onions, bunching onions never form a bulb — the entire plant from leaf tip to root is used, and cutting stems back to 1–2 inches above soil level promotes regrowth for a cut-and-come-again harvest cycle. Clumps can be divided every year or two to rejuvenate growth; older clumps become crowded and produce thinner stalks. In zone 9 they overwinter reliably and often survive light freezes, making them a year-round perennial green-onion source.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Onion Production in Florida
Cornell University Cooperative Extension — Bunching Onions

planting.note: In zone 9 direct sow September through November for a fall and winter harvest, or February through March for a brief spring crop before heat causes bolting. Chinese broccoli tolerates light frost but bolts quickly in temperatures above 75°F; the fall planting window is the primary season for the Gulf Coast.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun during the cool season; Chinese broccoli is more heat-tolerant than heading broccoli but still performs best between 50–70°F. Harvest the central flower head and 6–8 inches of stem just as the first buds show color but before flowers open; side shoots continue to develop for weeks of successive harvest. The stems are sweet and tender when young — the entire harvested shoot including leaves and stem is eaten, typically stir-fried with oyster sauce in Cantonese cuisine. Mulch to retain moisture and keep roots cool; consistent watering prevents the stems from becoming fibrous.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Brassica Crops
University of California Cooperative Extension — Chinese Broccoli
Asian Vegetable Research and Development Center — Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra

planting.note: Direct sow after all frost danger has passed and soil is warm; in zone 9 this is February–March or July–August. Purslane is an edible succulent that thrives in poor, dry soil where little else grows, making it a valuable summer green. It self-seeds prolifically once established.

directions: Direct sow seeds on the surface of poor to moderately fertile, well-drained soil — do not bury them, as they require light to germinate. Purslane is one of the most nutritious leafy greens available, with the highest plant-based omega-3 fatty acid (alpha-linolenic acid) content of any vegetable, plus high levels of vitamins C and E, beta-carotene, and glutathione. Harvest the succulent stem tips and young leaves when plants are 3–4 inches tall; the plants regrow vigorously after cutting. The mild, slightly lemony flavor works raw in salads (a staple in Turkish cacik and Middle Eastern fattoush), cooked in stews, or pickled. Remove plants before they set seed if you don't want them naturalizing throughout the garden.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Purslane as a Vegetable

planting.note: Sow in summer (August–September in zone 9) to grow roots by fall; the roots are then lifted and forced in darkness to produce the pale, blanched chicons called Belgian endive. In mild-winter zones 8–9 the roots can also be left in the ground to sprout blanched chicons naturally in winter if covered with soil.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in deep, loose, fertile soil; witloof chicory develops a long taproot like a parsnip that must be carefully dug in fall. To produce authentic Belgian endive chicons, lift the roots after tops die back, trim leaves to 1 inch, and force the roots upright in moist sand or potting mix in a dark, cool location (50–60°F) for 3–4 weeks until compact, pale yellow-white chicons 4–6 inches long emerge. The chicons have a pleasantly bitter flavor that mellows when cooked — they are traditionally braised, grilled, or wrapped in ham and baked with béchamel. In zone 9 the summer heat makes root development challenging; the fall-sow schedule is critical to success.
sources: Cornell University Cooperative Extension — Belgian Endive Production
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Chicory
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Witloof Chicory Growing Guide
